exemplar.title:
To what extent did the implementation of the EDSA Bus Carousel reduce  
negative externalities of car usage along EDSA from 2019 to 2023?

detailedExemplarData.title:
To what extent did the implementation of the EDSA Bus Carousel reduce  
negative externalities of car usage along EDSA from 2019 to 2023?

subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Title clearly frames the investigationâs scope and economic context
Research question is precise, narrowly focused, and directly connected to subsequent analysis
weaknesses: Methodology relies solely on secondary data with no critical evaluation of source reliability
Limited discussion of potential biases or alternative data-collection approaches
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work communicates its topic and focus very effectively and poses a clear, wellâframed research question (bands 5â6). However, the methodology, while appropriate in source selection, lacks depth in its treatment of data reliability and alternative methods (band 3â4). Overall, the criterion is met at the upper midârange, justifying a score of 4 out of 6.

strengths: Economic terminology and concepts are used accurately and consistently
Most data tables, graphs, and quotations are relevant and well integrated into the argument
weaknesses: Some theoretical explanations remain at a textbook level with occasional inaccuracies (e.g., MSC vs. supply curve)
A few sources are dated or only loosely connected to the core analysis, reducing full relevance
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates clear knowledge of economic concepts and generally applies source material appropriately (band 3â4). Occasional inaccuracies and the use of some dated or loosely linked data limit the performance, placing this criterion solidly in the upper middle band, warranting 4 out of 6.

strengths: Majority of research sources are appropriate and directly relevant to the research question
Clear linking of economic theory to the case study and acknowledgement of counterâpoints (e.g., Libreng Sakay)
Evidence of critical thinking in evaluation and recommendation sections
weaknesses: Analysis lacks depth in addressing confounding factors (e.g., COVID-19) and statistical rigor
No triangulation or systematic plan to assess data quality and potential biases
Evaluation of evidence is uneven with limited exploration of alternative explanations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation shows clear analysis and some well-supported conclusions (band 4â6), but causal claims are not rigorously tested and evaluation is only partially critical. Despite strengths in argumentation, the overall depth and critical appraisal remain moderate, meriting 4 out of 12.

strengths: The reportâs structure follows EE conventions with coherent headings and logical flow
weaknesses: Inconsistent layout and formatting (tables overrun margins, variable caption styles)
Page numbering is missing or hard to locate; the table of contents lacks accurate page references
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student employs an appropriate overall structure (band 3â4) but lapses in layout and formatting conventions (band 1â2) limit this criterion to the lower middle band. A score of 2 out of 4 reflects clear organization tempered by presentation inconsistencies.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question â âTo what extent did the implementation of the EDSA Bus Carousel reduce negative externalities of car usage along EDSA from 2019 to 2023?â â is explicitly stated, narrowly framed and strongly connected to the discussion that follows (traffic volume, emissions, accidents).  It is neither vague nor overly broad, allowing targeted data analysis.

The student states a precise topic â the effect of the EDSA Bus Carousel on the negative externalities of car use â and limits the geographical (EDSA) and temporal (2019-2023) scope. The introduction signals why the issue matters (congestion costs, emissions) and links directly to the discipline of Economics through the lens of market failure, making the purpose and focus clear and appropriate for a systematic investigation.

Most data tables, graphs and quotations are relevant to the question (traffic counts, PM10, fuel economy), and they are generally integrated into the argument.  Nonetheless, some sources are descriptive or taken at face value, and a few citations (e.g., fuel figures from Clean Air Asia) are dated or only loosely linked to the core analysis, demonstrating âmostlyâ rather than âconsistentlyâ appropriate application.

The essay follows the EE convention with title page, contents, sequential headings, introduction-method-analysis-conclusion, bibliography, and integrates tables/figures logically.  Minor structural issues (some repetition, very long analysis section) prevent it from being flawless but overall structure is clearly appropriate.

Formatting is generally acceptable (word count, title, tables, figures) but inconsistencies exist: some tables run over margins, figure captions vary, and page numbering is missing in the excerpt.  These lapses suggest only âsomeâ layout considerations are correctly applied rather than full adherence.

An argument is present (bus carousel mitigates externalities) and counter-points are acknowledged (termination of Libreng Sakay, rising registrations).  Recommendations and an evaluation section demonstrate some critical stance.  However, weighing of evidence is uneven and alternative explanations are not fully explored, so evaluation is only partially critical.

Analysis goes beyond description: the student compares bus vs. car fuel intensity, performs a basic sensitivity check, and interprets accident and PM10 trends.  Yet adjustments for confounding factors are superficial, calculations sometimes rely on broad averages, and causal links are asserted rather than rigorously tested, limiting depth.

The essay shows clear knowledge of negative externalities, marginal cost curves and common-access resources, and employs these ideas to frame the case study.  However, theoretical explanations sometimes remain textbook-level with limited depth, and occasional inaccuracies (equating MSC with supply in Fig. 2) indicate that understanding, while sound, is not fully coherent.

The majority of the evidence chosen (traffic volumes, accident data, emissions) is relevant and appropriate, showing logical connection to the RQ.  Gaps remain â no primary surveys, limited control for Covid-19 disruptions â so research quality is solid but not outstanding.

The student relies exclusively on secondary sources (MMDA, JICA, PSA, journal articles) and explains why secondary data are used, indicating some informed selection.  However, the range is moderate (principally government statistics plus one academic data set) and there is limited critical comment on reliability or alternative methods; no primary data, interviews or triangulation are attempted.  Thus methodology is adequate but not fully developed.

Economic terminology such as âdeadweight lossâ, âMPBâ, âMSCâ, âprice inelasticâ, and âsensitivity analysisâ is used accurately and consistently through text and diagrams.  Minor lapses (e.g., mismatched subscripts) do not seriously detract from clarity, so terminology quality aligns with the upper end of the middle band.

annotations.comment: Figure 2 lacks axis labels and magnitude indicators for the MPB shift, reducing the clarity of your visual argument.
The table of contents lacks page numbers, impairing navigability; include accurate page references for all sections.
Data on economic losses from 2017 may not fully reflect current trends; consider integrating more recent figures to maintain relevance.
The methodology section outlines secondary data sources but omits discussion of their reliability or potential biases; include critical evaluation.
No triangulation or validation plan is described for the secondary data; consider discussing how you will assess data quality and consistency.
The definition of market failure is accurate but generic; incorporating a specific example related to EDSAâs congestion would deepen application.
The local context section effectively situates EDSA as a case study; strengthening with the latest congestion metrics would enhance relevance.
Figure 1âs axes lack units and clear labels, making the marginal cost curves difficult to interpret accurately.
The text describes MSC as a supply curve in Figure 1; clarify the distinction between marginal social cost and supply for theoretical accuracy.
The assumption that MPB equals MSB requires justification; discuss potential external benefits or disbenefits not captured by private users.
The intervention section adeptly links economic theory to the EDSA Bus Carousel case, demonstrating clear critical thinking.
The title clearly communicates the investigationâs scope and focus on EDSA and negative externalities, effectively framing the research within an economic context.
The research question is precisely stated, narrowly framed, and well connected to the essayâs subsequent discussion on traffic, emissions, and accidents.
The word count of 3,428 is within the 4,000-word limit, demonstrating effective adherence to formatting requirements.
Page numbering appears inconsistently in the footer and is difficult to locate; adjust layout so page numbers are uniform and visible.
The introductory quote is engaging but lacks a precise source date or page number; provide a full citation to bolster credibility.
The traffic volume table spans years including the pandemic, but does not footnote Covid-19âs impact on 2020; add a clear annotation.
Your narrative of volume trends is descriptive; incorporating percent-change calculations or simple regression would deepen analysis.
The pandemicâs effect on car traffic is noted but not quantified; include percentage mobility declines to strengthen the causal argument.
The diagram on registered cars is clearly captioned, but consider discussing the reliability and potential biases of Statista data.
The Libreng Sakay section links policy changes to ridership shifts effectively, demonstrating a clear connection between incentives and behavior.
The accident data table is informative but comparing raw totals risks misleading; normalize by traffic volume or vehicle counts for accuracy.
The claim of reduced accidents is promising; including percentage change or statistical significance would better substantiate this conclusion.
The before-and-after busway component table is detailed, but summarizing key contrasts in the narrative would improve reader comprehension.
The fuel economy tables use a small sample of seven drivers; discuss sampling limitations and potential bias to clarify representativeness.
